coredata.dc:title: Surface and interface engineering of hierarchical photocatalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Rongchen
Jiang, Chuanjia
Xiang, Quanjun
Xie, Jun
Li, Xin
coredata.dc:description: In developing efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts, the design and fabrication of hierarchical semiconductors at the micro/nanometer scales have received much attention during the past decade due to their unique advantages in addressing the critical problems during photocatalysis. However, there are still many challenges in designing and constructing highly efficient hierarchical photocatalysts. Thus, in this review, we first systematically summarize and discusse the fundamentals and important interface engineering strategies of designing hierarchical photocatalysts, such as fabricating Z-Scheme heterojunctions, constructing Schottky-based heterojunctions, creating carbon-based heterojunctions and designing multicomponent heterojunctions. Then, especially, the different surface modification approaches of hierarchical porous photocatalysts, including loading cocatalysts, exposing the reactive facets, introducing defects/heteroatoms, adding photosensitizers, are highlighted. Finally, the major conclusions are made regarding this promising class of heterogeneous photocatalysts, and some perspectives are given on its future development. Through studying the important advances on this topic, it may pave a new avenue for fabricating highly effective hierarchical semiconductors for various applications in photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, thermal catalysis and other fields.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The advantages, challenges, interface engineering strategies and surface modification approaches of hierarchical photocatalysts are systematically summarized and addressed.

In developing efficient heterogeneous photocatalysts, the design and fabrication of hierarchical semiconductors at the micro/nanometer scales have received much attention during the past decade due to their unique advantages in addressing the critical problems during photocatalysis. However, there are still many challenges in designing and constructing highly efficient hierarchical photocatalysts. Thus, in this review, we first systematically summarize and discusse the fundamentals and important interface engineering strategies of designing hierarchical photocatalysts, such as fabricating Z-Scheme heterojunctions, constructing Schottky-based heterojunctions, creating carbon-based heterojunctions and designing multicomponent heterojunctions. Then, especially, the different surface modification approaches of hierarchical porous photocatalysts, including loading cocatalysts, exposing the reactive facets, introducing defects/heteroatoms, adding photosensitizers, are highlighted. Finally, the major conclusions are made regarding this promising class of heterogeneous photocatalysts, and some perspectives are given on its future development. Through studying the important advances on this topic, it may pave a new avenue for fabricating highly effective hierarchical semiconductors for various applications in photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, thermal catalysis and other fields.

ce:section.ce:para: As an appealing technology, heterogeneous photocatalysis, by means of solar energy absorption and utilization over semiconductors, has recently gained considerable attention in the fields of materials science, energy and environmental science, and chemistry [1–7]. Since the first report of Honda–Fujishima effect of TiO2 photoelectrode in 1972 [8], many progresses in exploiting novel semiconductor materials and derivatives, including oxides of metals with d0 and d10 configuration [3,7,9–12], sulfides [13–17], (oxy)nitride [9,18–22], metal-free semiconductors [23–31], plasmonic metals [32–38] and elemental photocatalysts [39–43] have been achieved for different applications [18,44–50]. However, none of these photocatalysts could satisfy all practical requirements such as long lifetime of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, small band gaps for the full utilization of solar light energy, relatively high safety, low cost, high efficiency and stability, thus greatly hindering their practical applications [7]. Consequently, a wide variety of methods have been proposed to boost the overall photocatalysis efficiency of various kinds of photocatalysts through shape-, morphology-, composition-, surface-, interface- and structure-engineering strategies. Among them, fabrication of hierarchical porous photocatalysts through the self-assembly of nanometer-scaled building blocks have been found to be a promising strategy to simultaneously prevent aggregation of nano-blocks, enhance the efficiencies of light harvesting and reactant adsorption, and improve the transport of guest species to framework binding sites, thus significantly boosting the overall quantum efficiency of the specific photocatalyst.

To date, a range of important research papers and reviews have been published on design, fabrication strategies and functional applications of the hierarchical porous photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic efficiency [120–123]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is still no review toward further improving the photoactivity of hierarchical porous photocatalysts through proper surface and interface modification strategies. Thus, it is timely and necessary to present a new comprehensive review on this subject, which will promote further developments in this important area of research. In this review, we will focus on the interfacial engineering and surface modification of hierarchical porous photocatalysts. Through studying the important advances in this topic, it may open up new opportunities for designing highly effective hierarchical porous photocatalysts for various photocatalysis applications.

So far, fundamentals of heterogeneous photocatalysis have been widely proposed [7,23,120,124–129]. The typical photocatalytic mechanism can be presented in Fig. 3 . As shown in Fig. 3, the heterogeneous photocatalytic processes generally consist of five consecutive steps: (1) light harvesting; (2) charge excitation/separation; (3) charge migration/transport; (4) interfacial charge transfer and (5) charge utilization (surface electrocatalysis). The overall efficiency of photocatalysis is dependent on all factors that can affect each of the five stages. From the point of view of practical use, all factors affecting the above five stages should be simultaneously improved to further enhance and optimize the photocatalysis processes. Additionally, the undesired charge recombination in bulk and on the surface of semiconductors should be minimized. Straightly speaking, improving the bulk and surface charge separation has been demonstrated to be one of the most important issues for boosting the overall photocatalytic efficiency [126,130–133]. In this end, various heterostructured photocatalysts with the built-in electrical fields have been extensively constructed to fundamentally promote the bulk and surface charge separation during the past decades [126,130–133].

To construct better built-in electrical fields for fundamentally boosting interfacial charge separation, it is necessary to deeply understand the space charge layer formation and band bending on the surface of semiconductors [131]. In general, a space charge layer could be created by the transfer and trapping of charge carriers at the surface/interface between the semiconductor and the contact phase (i.e., liquid, gas, or metal), thus leading to the favorable band bending [125,134]. Clearly, for n-type semiconductors, there is no band bending in the absence of a space charge layer (Fig. 4 ). An accumulation layer with the bands bending downward near the surface of n-type semiconductors could be formed by a positive charge on the interface (Fig. 4), due to the increased majority carrier concentration of electrons in the surface region. By contrast, a depletion layer with the bands bend upward near the surface of n-type semiconductors could be formed when the surface is negatively charged, owing to the decreased surface majority electron carrier concentration (Fig. 4). Additionally, an inversion layer with surface p-type behavior could be formed for a n-type semiconductor through extending the depletion of the majority charge carriers far into the semiconductor because of the significantly decreased Fermi level below the intrinsic level (Fig. 4). In a word, the space charge layer formation and band bending are of great importance for understanding the fabrication of heterojunctions and favorable charge separation.

Impressively, fabricating hierarchical photocatalysts has been proven to be an appealing strategy to simultaneously address almost all of the challenges at the five stages during the photocatalysis processes, as shown in Fig. 3. In other words, the hierarchical porous photocatalysts could effectively achieve the improved light harvesting, promoted charge transfer and separation, increased active surface area and reaction sites, minimized mass transfer barriers and suppressed aggregation of nano-blocks (Fig. 5 ). The detailed and illustrative analysis about the improved light harvesting, increased active surface area and reaction sites, and minimized mass transfer barriers has been given in our previous review [120]. Herein, the other two aspects of the advantages of hierarchical photocatalysts, i.e., promoted charge transfer and separation, and suppressed aggregation of nano-blocks, will be carefully addressed.

It is known that fast charge recombination and slow electron transfer in many semiconductors significantly limited their practical photocatalysis applications. Therefore, another important way to boost the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalysts is to promote the charge transfer and separation in the semiconductor crystals. As a key step in photocatalysis process, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs must be quickly separated and transferred to the surface before participating in the photocatalytic reactions. Clearly, in hierarchically structured photocatalysts, the nanosized effects of building blocks such as nanowires or nanosheets can facilitate the efficient migration and transfer of charge carriers generated inside the crystal to the surface, thus leading to suppressed recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes and the enhanced photocatalytic behaviors [135–138]. Furthermore, the unique morphological and structural features of the hierarchical micro/nanostructures provide a lower charge transfer resistance and a significantly shortened bulk diffusion length of charge carriers, which could restrain the possible recombination of electron-hole pairs and prolong lifetimes of the photogenerated charge carriers, thus favoring the efficient promotion of photocatalytic performances [139]. Moreover, the formed space charge layers in microspheres can promote the separation of electron-hole pairs, thus prolonging lifetimes of the photogenerated charge carriers [140]. In short, all these factors are partially beneficial for the enhancement in the separation and transfer of charge carriers, thus achieving improved photocatalytic properties of hierarchical photocatalysts. Nevertheless, the absence of built-in electric field significantly limits the rates of charge transfer and separation, which should be further improved in designing the high-quality hierarchical photocatalysts.

Compared with the common semiconductor nanoparticles, hierarchical photocatalysts could efficiently suppress the aggregation of nanosized building blocks [141–144]. It is known that the semiconductor nanoparticles often suffer from aggregation and deactivation because of their high surface energy. The aggregation greatly decreases the amount of exposed surface active sites and usually has a negative effect on the enhancement of photocatalytic performance. In contrast, the self-supporting 3D micro/nanostructures of hierarchical photocatalysts can effectively inhibit the severe aggregation of nanosized building blocks, thereby preserving the highly active sites on crystal planes with high interface energy, which may be conducive to improved photocatalytic activity.

In a word, the superior photocatalytic performance of the hierarchical porous micro/nanostructures can be ascribed to their unique structural features, which can suppress the aggregation of micro/nanoscale building blocks, increase surface areas and active reaction sites, decrease the mass diffusion barrier and promote the charge transfer rates, thus leading to a much stronger improvement in the photocatalytic activity. All these advantages of hierarchical nanoarchitectures have led to their widespread applications in the photocatalysis.

Various kinetics factors (such as charge carrier kinetics, surface reaction kinetics, adsorption and diffusion kinetics) have been long considered to be more critical and challenging than thermodynamic factors for determining the efficiency of photocatalysis [145–148]. Among these challenging kinetics factors, the complicated charge carrier separation dynamics and surface reaction kinetics are well accepted to be two most important factors that have often lead to low quantum efficiency in multi-step heterogeneous photocatalysis [149,150].

On the one hand, the quick recombination kinetics of photogenerated electron–hole pairs in the bulk or on the surface of hierarchical porous photocatalysts has become one of the most challenging issues in achieving high photocatalytic efficiency [7]. To address this challenge, constructing various heterojunctions through interface engineering has inspired many new strategies for boosting the charge separation and maximizing the photocatalytic efficiency of hierarchical porous photocatalysts [130,131]. In addition, the exposed crystal facets with high energy and heterojunction interfaces can also promote the photocatalysis reaction kinetics and charge-carrier separation, respectively, thus improving photocatalysis properties [151–154].

On the other hand, for the uphill reactions such as water splitting and CO2 reduction, the sluggish surface reaction kinetics could result in the accumulation of photogenerated electrons and holes on hierarchical porous photocatalysts, which would accelerate the undesired photocorrosion process of the photocatalysts, thus significantly decreasing their photoactivities [6,7]. Accordingly, different surface modification methods, such as loading suitable cocatalysts, exposing the reactive facets, introducing defects/heteroatoms, and adding photosensitizer, have been exploited to promote the surface charge separation and accelerate the surface reaction kinetics. The various available hierarchical heterojunctions and surface modification methods will be thoroughly discussed in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interface engineering of hierarchical porous photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: To further improve the charge separation efficiency, the creation of heterojunction interfaces has been widely proposed and studied during the past decade. In generally, according to the semiconductors’ energy bands and Femi levels of metal cocatalysts, heterojunctions can be classified into six types: Type II heterojunction, p-n heterojunction (Fig. 6 A), direct Z-Scheme heterojunction (Fig. 6B), Schottky-based heterojunction (Fig. 6C), carbon-based heterojunction (Fig. 6D) and multicomponent heterojunction. The typical examples for interface engineering of hierarchical photocatalysts are summarized in Table 1 . Clearly, the traditional Type II heterojunctions can also promote the fast spatial separation of electrons and holes, thus retarding their recombination and prolonging their lifetime. So far, a number of hierarchical photocatalysts with Type II heterojunctions have been widely reported for greatly enhanced photocatalysis [76,155–169]. Taking the hierarchical Bi-based heterojunctions as an example, a much higher photocatalytic activity could be realized over the Bi-based heterojunctions as compared to pure Bi-based photocatalysts [138,170,171]. For instance, the hierarchical heterojunctions between Bi2S3 and other semiconductors (such as Bi2O3 [172,173], Bi2WO6 [174–176], Bi4Ti3O12 [177], g-C3N4 [178], polyaniline (PANI) [179], BiOCl [180], (BiO)2CO3 [181–184] and WO3 [185]) have been extensively investigated, all of which exhibit greatly enhanced photocatalytic performances due to the improved charge separation.

However, there seems to be some controversy over the charge separation mechanism in Type II heterojunction [186]. According to the band bending and Femi level alignment, Type II heterojunction cannot be achieved under light illumination (Fig. 7 ). Moreover, the redox reactions are generally driven by the photogenerated electrons and holes with weaker reduction and oxidation potentials in Type II alignment, thus restricting their activity enhancement. In contrast, various other kinds of heterojunctions, such as p-n heterojunction, Z-Scheme heterojunction, Schottky-based heterojunction, carbon-based heterojunction and multicomponent heterojunction, have high potential for improving the photocatalytic activity over hierarchical photocatalysts.

The p–n heterojunction has been widely reported for the enhanced photocatalysis, whose formation mechanism is shown in Fig. 8
[186]. Under light irradiation, free electrons of n-type semiconductor can transfer to the contacted p-type semiconductor due to the larger work function of p-type semiconductor, forming a built-in electric field (Fig. 8b), which is favorable for accomplishing the p–n junction charge carrier transfer (Fig. 8c), instead of the direct Z-scheme charge carrier transfer (Fig. 8d). Thus, the direct Z-scheme transfer process of photogenerated charge carriers cannot be achieved between p-type and n-type semiconductors (Fig. 8c and d).

So far, various kinds of hierarchical p-n heterojunctions have been fabricated between n-type and p-type semiconductors, including porous flower-like NiO/TiO2 [240], NiO/ZnO microsphere [69], flower-like Co3O4/Bi2O2CO3 [188], CuO/ZnO [190], Sr2TiO4/BiO7 [241], Cu2O/ZnO hetero-nanobrush [242], Bi2O3-Bi2WO6 [243], BiOCl/ZnFe2O4 [244], Co3O4/CdS [245], BiFeO3@TiO2 [246], Cu2O/g-C3N4 [247,248], Ag3PO4/MoO3 [249], BiPO4/BiOBr [250], BiOI/TiO2 [251], m-Bi2O4/BiOCl [252], MoSe2/Bi2WO6 [253], Ag2O/BiOCOOH [254], BiVO4/BiOBr core-shell mesoporous spindles [54] and ring-shaped Bi2WO6@CeO2 [255]. Impressively, most of these hierarchical p-n heterojunctions were used in the photocatalytic degradation of various pollutants, such as methyl orange (MO), Rhodamine B (RhB), Congo red (CR) dye, cyanide, norfloxacin, p-chlorophenol and phenol. As a typical example, Yu et al. fabricated hierarchical porous flower-like NiO/TiO2 p–n junction microsphere (Fig. 9 A) through a solvothermal and deposition technology [240]. The resulting p–n junctions exhibit much higher photodegradation activity than conventional TiO2 powders and NiO superstructures toward p-chlorophenol aqueous solution (Fig. 9B). Importantly, the formation of NiO/TiO2 p–n junction can boost the separation rate of photogenerated electron–hole pairs, thus subsequently enhancing the photocatalytic activity (Fig. 9C). This study opens a new way for designing the complex hierarchical porous p–n junction architectures for enhanced photocatalytic activity. Similarly, Yang et al. fabricated hierarchical flower-like Co3O4/Bi2O2CO3 with p-n junction (Fig. 10 A) via a two-step hydrothermal process [188]. Among all Co3O4/Bi2O2CO3 samples with different Co3O4 contents, the 0.6 wt% Co3O4/Bi2O2CO3 exhibits the highest visible-light photoactivity for MO degradation, with the superoxide radicals (
O2 −) as the main active species (Fig. 10B). Importantly, it was proposed that the enhanced activity can be attributed to the formation of Co3O4/Bi2O2CO3 p-n junction (Fig. 10C), which could effectively accelerate the separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs and increase the concentration of
O2 − involving in the photodegradation process.

Additionally, Peng et al. constructed a novel hierarchical 1D Bi2O3 nanorod–Bi2WO6 nanosheet p–n junction photocatalyst by a three-step synthetic route [243]. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images confirmed that Bi2WO6 nanosheets vertically grew on the Bi2O3 rods with a diameter of about 200 nm (Fig. 11 A). The results demonstrate that the novel Bi2O3–Bi2WO6 p–n heterostructures display higher photocatalytic activity toward the degradation of RhB than single p-type Bi2O3 nanorods or n-type Bi2WO6 flowers (Fig. 11B). The total densities of the states of valence band (VB) confirmed that the VB maximum of WB2 (1.71 eV) is between those of Bi2O3 (1.3 eV) and Bi2WO6 (2.24 eV) (Fig. 11C). The results suggested that the formation of internal electrostatic field in the Bi2O3–Bi2WO6 p–n junction structures (Fig. 11D) could be beneficial for the effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus resulting in the significant enhancement of the photocatalytic activity. In addition, the improved transport of photogenerated charge carriers could partially favor the activity enhancement due to the formation of the 1D p–n heterostructures made of ordered nanosheets. This example further indicates the advanced applications of hierarchical p–n heterojunctions in different photocatalytic fields. Besides photodegradation, In et al. reported that, in photocatalytic CO2 reduction, the CH4 production rate over heterostructured CuO-TiO2-
x N x hollow nanocubes was 2.5 times higher than that of Degussa P25 TiO2 under the same experimental conditions, due to the increased absorbance below 500 nm and the formation of p-n junctions [256]. In future, it is expected that more and more hierarchical p–n heterojunctions could be applied in the photoreduction of CO2, water splitting and reduction of N2 [245,257].

In recent years, the hierarchical all-solid-state Z-scheme photocatalysts using different electron mediators have been widely reported [91,142,203,229,234,235,238,268,269]. However, as mentioned above, the high costs or the light shielding effects of charge carrier transfer mediators significantly limits their extensive applications. By contrast, the more advantageous direct Z-scheme photocatalysts have been rationally designed and constructed with hierarchical structures to maximize the photocatalytic efficiency. To date, numerous hierarchical direct Z-scheme photocatalysts have been available for different photocatalytic applications, including CdS-WO3 [201], ZnIn2S4 marigold flower/Bi2WO6 flower [192], Bi2O3/g-C3N4 [270], ZnO nanoparticles implanted in TiO2 macrochannels [271], PDA-modified ZnO [272], MoS2/BiOI/AgI [228], g-C3N4/ZnO microspheres [196], TiO2/g-C3N4 [198,273–276], WO3/g-C3N4 [68,277,278], TiO2/WO3 [279], Co3O4/g-C3N4 [280], BiVO4/g-C3N4 [281], g-C3N4/Bi2WO6 [282,283], PTCDA/g-C3N4 [284], Cu2V2O7/g-C3N4 [285], Bi2WO6/BiPO4 [286], g-C3N4/NiTiO3 [287], Bi2MoO6/BiOBr [288], WO3/CdS [289,290], Bi2O3/g-C3N4 [291], CdS/CdWO4 [292], BiOBr/ [273–275,293], BiOBr-Bi2MoO6 [294], Ag2CO3/N-doped graphene [295], WO3/Ag3PO4 [296], Fe2O3/g-C3N4 [81], g-C3N4/CuInS2 [202], BiOI/MIL-88B(Fe) [297], TiO2/CdS [197], nanotree-like CdS/ZnO [298], Fe3O4@MoS2/Ag3PO4 [200], and SnSe2/Ag3PO4 [204]. As a typical example, the hierarchical direct Z-scheme CdS–WO3 hollow spheres were successfully constructed via the self-assembly of numerous small monoclinic WO3 nanosheets of about 30 nm thickness and 4–8 nm CdS nanoparticles (Fig. 14 A–C) [201]. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image confirms the intimate interface between WO3 nanosheets and CdS nanoparticles (Fig. 14D). The hierarchical CdS–WO3 composites exhibited enhanced photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity than pure CdS and WO3, and the highest CH4-generation rate could be achieved over the CdS–WO3 heterostructures with a nominal CdS/(WO3 + CdS) molar fraction of 5% (Fig. 14E). The increase of hydroxyl radicals (
OH) concentration, as measured using coumarin-assited photoluminescence (PL) technique, over CdS–WO3 heterostructures confirmed the direct Z-scheme mechanisms, which could significantly boost charge separation and increase the redox ability of photogenerated electrons and holes (Fig. 14F), thus leading to the enhanced photoactivity. This study highlights the promising application of high-performance direct Z-scheme photocatalysts in CO2 photoreduction. Similarly, Nie et al. recently prepared the hierarchical g-C3N4/ZnO composite microsphere with intimate contact interface by electrostatic self-assembly of negatively charged g-C3N4 and positively charged ZnO (Fig. 15 A and B) [196]. The optimized g-C3N4/ZnO composite could achieve higher CH3OH production rate than the pristine ZnO and g-C3N4 (Fig. 15C). The calculated work functions of g-C3N4 and ZnO (4.67 and 5.15 eV, respectively) (Fig. 15D and E) are beneficial for the formation of the built-in electric field, and the electrons transfer from the conduction band (CB) of ZnO and recombination with holes in the VB of g-C3N4 according to the direct Z-scheme mechanism (Fig. 15F), as evidenced by the time-dependent
OH radical generation. These two works highlight the excellent synergistic effect of hierarchical structure and direct Z-scheme charge-separation in photocatalytic CO2 reduction reactions.

Besides CO2 photoreduction, the hierarchical direct Z-scheme photocatalysts have been also applied in the photocatalytic H2 production. For instance, He et al. successfully synthesized the novel hierarchical 1D/2D WO3/g-C3N4/Ni(OH) x hybrids via a two-step strategy: high-temperature calcination and subsequent in-situ photodeposition (Fig. 16 A) [193]. The optimum photocatalytic activity of CW20 (containing 20 wt% defective WO3 nanorods) photocatalyst with 4.8 wt% Ni(OH) x exhibited the highest H2-production rate of 576 μmol g−1 h−1, which is 5.7, 10.8 and 230 times higher than those of g-C3N4/4.8 wt% Ni(OH) x , CW20 and g-C3N4 photocatalysts, respectively (Fig. 16B). The increased concentrations of
O2 − and
OH radicals could further reveal the direct Z-scheme charge separation mechanism (Fig. 16C), which could effectively inhibit the recombination of the photoexcited electron-hole pairs of g-C3N4 and WO3, and boost the photocatalytic H2 evolution over Ni(OH) x cocatalysts (Fig. 16D). This study may provide more insights into constructing highly robust direct Z-scheme heterojunction materials for various photocatalytic applications. similarly, Meng et al. fabricated a direct Z-scheme TiO2/CdS hierarchical photocatalyst via a successive ionic layer adsorption and reaction (SILAR) technique (Fig. 17 A and B) [197]. The as-prepared TiO2/CdS hybrid photocatalyst exhibited noticeably promoted photocatalytic H2-production activity of 51.4 μmol h−1 due to the hierarchical structure and the efficient interfacial charge separation and migration (Fig. 17C). The calculated work functions of the TiO2 (1 0 1) and CdS (1 1 0) planes (7.1 and 5.8 eV, respectively) (Fig. 17D and E), as well as the PL analysis of
OH radicals were performed to further confirm the direct Z-scheme mechanism (Fig. 17F), which could suppress the electron-hole recombination of TiO2 and CdS and maintain the strong electron reducibility in CdS CB for enhancing the photocatalytic H2 evolution. Moreover, Yu and coworkers constructed a direct Z-scheme CdS/cobalt-benzimidazole (Co-bIm) coordination polymer (CBP) biomimetic photocatalyst (Fig. 18 A) [195]. Without any noble metal cocatalyst the optimal H2-evolution rate for CdS/CBP photocatalysts could reach 488 μmol h−1, which was 10.6 times higher than that of pure CdS due to the intimate interfacial contact layer between the CdS and the CBP (Fig. 18B). The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis confirmed that the photogenerated holes are accumulated on the VB of CdS upon charge transfer (Fig. 18C and D). The transient photocurrent response of CdS-CPB further confirmed the efficient charge separation (Fig. 18E). These results suggest that the Z-scheme heterojunctions between CdS and CPB (Fig. 18F) are beneficial for the efficient charge separation, thus achieving superior photocatalytic activity. Yu et al. synthesized the direct Z-scheme g-C3N4/WO3 photocatalyst for boosted photocatalytic H2 production, where the 2D g-C3N4 layers stand vertically on the flat facets of WO3 nanocuboids (Fig. 19 A) [194]. The optimal H2-evolution rate for g-C3N4/WO3-Pt could reach 3.12 mmol h−1 g−1, which was 7.09 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4 (Fig. 19B). Through both experimental and theoretical investigations, mechanistic insights regarding the direct Z-scheme electron transfer from WO3 to g-C3N4 were obtained. According to the energy band alignment between g-C3N4 and WO3, the Fermi energy level of g-C3N4 is higher than that of WO3. An internal electric field from g-C3N4 (+) to WO3 (−) will be generated due to Fermi level equilibrium between g-C3N4 and WO3. As a result, the energy bands of g-C3N4 and WO3 phases will bend up and down at their interface, respectively. Therefore, under light excitation the opposite band bending could enhance the recombination between the electrons in the CB of WO3 and the holes in the VB of g-C3N4, thus driving the direct Z-scheme charge transfer without the aid of any electron transfer mediator (Fig. 19C and D). Based on this proposed structure, density functional theory (DFT) calculations of the g-C3N4/WO3 interface revealed that electron transfer was driven by the covalent W-O-N-(C)2 interaction (Fig. 19E).

Additionally, the hierarchical direct Z-scheme photocatalysts have also been applied in the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. Recently, Guo et al. prepared the novel hierarchical Fe3O4@MoS2/Ag3PO4 magnetic nanophotocatalyst by simply depositing Ag3PO4 onto the surface of crumpled Fe3O4@MoS2 nanosphere [200]. The results confirmed that Fe3O4@MoS2/Ag3PO4 with 6 wt% Ag3PO4 (FM/A-6%) showed the highest photocatalytic degradation activity toward CR and RhB under both visible light and simulated sunlight irradiation (Fig. 20 A and B). The radical trapping experiments in the presence of ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (h+ scavenger), p-benzoquinone (
O2 − scavenger), and isopropanol (
OH scavenger) confirmed that the active species affect the photocatalytic activity in the following order: h+ >
O2 − >
OH (Fig. 20C). Considering the VB and CB positions of Ag3PO4 and MoS2 (Fig. 20D), the direct Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism between MoS2 and Ag3PO4 could be further proposed, instead of traditional type II charge transfer mechanism (Fig. 20E and F).

It is expected that more and more hierarchical direct Z-scheme photocatalysts could be rationally fabricated for various photocatalytic applications. Importantly, besides radical species trapping test, more direct evidence for the direct Z-scheme charge separation mechanism should be provided in future studies, such as metal loading, XPS characterization [299], DFT calculation [196,262] and internal electric-field simulation [129,132,186,258] Additionally, optimizing the pH value, cocatalyst, doping, architecture and mass ratio could be further used to enhance the activity of the third-generation direct Z-scheme photocatalysts [129].

Typically, for the semiconductor-metal system, due to the higher work function (ϕ m) of metal than that of semiconductor (ϕ s), the photogenerated electrons will migrate from the semiconductor to the metal to align the two Fermi levels, thus resulting in the formation of the Schottky barrier with a depletion layer and upward band bending (Fig. 21 ) [300]. The Schottky-based heterojunction as an efficient electron trap could promote the electron-hole separation in photocatalysis [125,126].

So far, various kinds of hierarchical Schottky-based heterojunction (such as Pt/ZnO [209,301], BiWO6/Pd [302], Bi/Bi2WO6 [303,304], Au/CdS [305], g-C3N4/Ag [208], TiO2/Ag [210]) have been widely used in photocatalytic H2 evolution and pollutant degradation. When semiconductor photocatalyst was combined with metal, the electrons with high work function will easily move from the CB of hierarchical semiconductor photocatalysts to metal with lower work function until two Fermi energy levels equilibrate. This forms a space charge layer on the surface of semiconductor, thus leading to highly efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transfer of the charge carriers. For example, Yang and coworkers demonstrated that the hierarchical Ag/N-TiO2 Schottky-based heterojunction displayed a higher H2-evolution rate (4.7 mmol h−1 g−1) than pure N-TiO2 (2.1 mmol h−1 g−1) under visible light for ethanol-splitting (Fig. 22 A). It is believed that Ag cannot only trap electrons but also form the Schottky barrier, thus leading to highly efficient separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and transfer of carriers during the alcohol photosplitting reaction (Fig. 22B and C) [210].

In another example, it was demonstrated that Ag-doped ZnO nanorod (Fig. 23 A) also showed excellent photocatalytic degradation performance under visible light [211]. The maximum rate constant k value of Ag/ZnO was 0.02789 min−1, which was three times higher than that of pure ZnO (0.00967 min−1) (Fig. 23B and C), due to enhanced visible light absorption and red shift in the absorption region (Fig. 23D), thus leading to enhanced generation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. It is consistent with the photocurrent results (Fig. 23E). Furthermore, the formation of Ag/ZnO Schottky-based heterojunction interface could improve the transport of photogenerated electrons from ZnO nanorod under visible light irradiation (Fig. 23F).

Here, it should be noted that some metals, such as Au, Ag, and Bi, could exhibit the surface plasmons, which could extend the light absorption range over the full solar spectrum [105,303,306–314]. Clearly, both the plasmonic metals and hierarchical structures could improve the light harvesting, thus achieving the photocatalytic enhancement. Thus, it is expected that the photocatalysis could be fundamentally enhanced by the combination of the plasmonic effects, Schottky-based heterojunction and hierarchical structures. In fact, the Schottky-based heterojunctions between plasmonic metals and hierarchical semiconductors have been extensively constructed to boost the visible-light photocatalysis [36,315–317]. In future, it is anticipated that the more and more plasmonic metals could be applied in improving the photocatalysis over different hierarchical semiconductors.

As is well known, metallic nanocarbons (i.e., carbon quantum dots, carbon nanotube, graphene and carbon fiber) have been employed as the excellent promoters to boost the photocatalysis over various semiconductors, due to their unique electronic conductivity, low costs and toxicity, superior adsorption performance, and high surface area and stability [17,126,127,318–328]. Hierarchical nanocarbon-supported semiconductors (such as graphene/CdS nanorods [329], graphene/g-C3N4 [330], MoS2/graphene [214], WS2/graphene/CdS nanorods [220], MoS2/graphene/ZnIn2S4 [213], Cu2MoS4 hollow sphere/graphene [222], BiOI/RGO [331], TiO2 nanorod/RGO [332], N-(BiO)2CO3/graphene [333], carbon/ZnO [334], TiO2-graphene multiple-layered MoS2 [227], Ni3S2/graphene [335] and graphitic carbon/TiO2 [336–340]) have been employed to improve CO2 adsorption and promote the charge transfer and separation, thus resulting in the activity enhancement in CO2 photoconversion. Xu and coworkers demonstrated that the CdS nanowires–reduced graphene oxide (RGO) nanoheterojunctions exhibit significantly enhanced photoactivity for selective reduction of nitro organics to amino organics in water under visible light irradiation [341]. It is believed that the addition of RGO can not only efficiently enhance charge carrier transfer and separation, but also greatly improve the adsorption capacity for aromatic nitro organics, thus leading to the drastic photoactivity improvement. In another example, it was demonstrated that the 1D CdS nanowires-carbon nanotubes nanocomposites exhibit remarkably enhanced photoactivity toward selective reduction of aromatic nitro organics in water under visible light irradiation [342]. It is believed that the integrated factors of the unique 1D–1D carbon-based heterojunction, the suppressed charge-carrier recombination, and the enhanced visible-light absorption lead to the significantly enhanced photoactivity.

In addition, the photoactivities of hierarchical Bi-based photocatalysts could be also enhanced through hybridization with 2D graphene [170,343–347]. It is highly expected that more and more hierarchical Bi-based photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic performances could be designed and developed [138,348–350]. For example, Ke et al. successfully synthesized 3D hierarchical RGO/Bi2MoO6 photocatalyst for improving photocatalytic water oxidation. The result indicated that combining RGO with Bi2MoO6 could improve photocatalytic water oxidation and organic pollutant degradation [219]. The rate of O2 generation was 6 μmol L−1 h−1, 1.8 times higher than that of the pure Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 24 A). Moreover, the performance of photocatalytic RhB degradation shows similar results (Fig. 24B). The RGO/Bi2MoO6 could completely degrade RhB in two hours under solar light irradiation, which is 1.4 times higher than that of pure Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 24B). The improvement mechanism of photocatalytic activity of carbon-based heterojunctions is shown in Fig. 24C.

Jin and coworkers successfully synthesized GO covered N-doped (BiO)2CO3 via a hydrothermal approach (Fig. 25 A and B) [351]. The N-doped (BiO)2CO3 with 1.0 wt%GO exhibited the highest NO removal of 62%, which is 3.6 and 2.2 times higher than those of (BiO)2CO3 and N-doped (BiO)2CO3 photocatalysts, respectively (Fig. 25C). The UV–vis absorption of GO@(BiO)2CO3 not only displayed a red shift, but also showed a stronger absorbance in the visible light region (Fig. 25D), which suggested that the optical property of N-doped (BiO)2CO3 was significantly enhanced by the introduction of GO. As a result, under visible light irradiation, the N-doped (BiO)2CO3 was excited to create the photogenerated electrons and holes. Then the electrons were transferred from the CB of (BiO)2CO3 to GO due to the excellent conductivity of GO and formation of carbon-based heterojunctions, which prolongs the lifetime of photogenerated electrons (Fig. 25E).

Wang et al. have demonstrated that the photocatalytic degradation performance of TiO2 could be improved by loading appropriate amounts of graphene [216]. The macro/mesoporous TiO2 with a low loading (0.05 wt%) of graphene exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity, which is 1.7 and 1.6 times higher than that of pure TiO2 and P25, respectively (Fig. 26 A). The improved photocatalytic performance is due to the excellent conductivity of graphene, thus improving the separation of charge carriers and boosting the photocatalytic performance (Fig. 26B). The transient photocurrent response experiment further confirmed the improved photocatalytic performance. Under UV light, the TiO2 was excited to create photogenerated electrons and holes. Then the electrons were transferred from the CB of TiO2 to the graphene due to the excellent conductivity of graphene. The holes would combine with adsorbed H2O molecules or hydroxide anion to form
OH, while the electrons stored in graphene could react with O2 to form
O2 −. Finally, the
OH and
O2 react with acetone (Fig. 26C) [216].

RGO-covered WO3 hierarchical heterojunctions also showed excellent photocatalytic activity in degrading RhB (Fig. 27 A) [212]. After wrapping by RGO, the WO3/RGO photocatalysts showed strong EPR signals for
OH and
O2 − (Fig. 27B and C), which suggest that the WO3/RGO photocatalyst is favorable for the generation of
OH and
O2 −, thus leading to the improved photocatalytic activity. The WO3 with 1.2 wt% RGO showed the optimal performance, which was 2.5 times higher than that of the pure WO3 [212] (Fig. 27D). The improved photocatalytic activity could also be further confirmed by photocurrent response and UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 27E and F).

Liu and coworkers constructed metal oxide/carbon heterojunctions for photocatalytic activity in methylene blue (MB) degradation [217]. According to UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) analysis, P25 showed little light absorbance in the UV region (Fig. 28 A). In contrast, TiO2/C heterojunction photocatalysts maintain high photon absorption in the visible region, which is attributed to the presence of C in the TiO2 matrix (Fig. 28B and C). Furthermore, after coupling with carbon, the surface area of the composite material has an obvious increase. These results demonstrated that the homogeneous distribution of C in TiO2 acts as an efficient electron transfer site to increase the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. Therefore, the photocatalytic activity in MB degradation for TiO2/C heterojunction is 2.2 times higher than that of P25 (Fig. 28D). Therefore, combining carbon with metal oxide could lead to high surface area for ion transport and trapping and high electrical conductivity for charge carrier separation (Fig. 28E). In future, it is expected that other kinds of carbon materials, such as carbon quantums, nanotubes, nanofibers and microspheres could be utilized to construct the advanced carbon-based heterojunctions for the boosted photocatalysis.

Moreover, the utilization of visible light, charge carrier separation and transfer, and surface photocatalytic reactions can improved by fabricating multicomponent heterojunctions formed between more than two components. More specifically, the synergetic improvement of the charge separation and transfer is mainly caused by the different interfacial electrical fields in multicomponent heterojunction, thus reaching the significantly enhanced photocatalytic efficiency. Typically, integrating Schottky junctions into Type II heterojunctions/Z-scheme heterojunctions could fundanmentally prolong the lifetime of photogenerated electrons, thus improving their photocatalytic performance. To date, various multicomponent heterojunctions, such as Ag@TiO2@ZnO [352], Ag@AgBr/BiPO4/r-GO [229], TiO2@MoS2@CdS [353], Ag2S-coupled ZnO@ZnS core–shell nanorod [53] and MoS2/BiOI/AgI [228] have been widely reported. For instance, Ding and coworkers successfully synthesized the Ag@AgBr/BiPO4/r-GO multicomponent heterojunctions via an in situ deposition and photoreduction method [229]. The result conformed that the Ag@AgBr/BiPO4/r-GO multicomponent heterojunction showed much more excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of RB-19 than other samples under visible light irradiation (Fig. 29 A and B). Ag@AgBr/BiPO4/r-GO multicomponent heterojunctions show stronger photocurrent intensity than that of pure BiPO4, suggesting that the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs were significantly enhanced [229]. Under visible light irradiation, the AgBr and BiPO4 were excited and generated electron-hole pairs. First, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of BiPO4 would transfer to the VB of AgBr through Z-scheme heterojunctions, then to the surface of Ag NPs and r-GO through Schottky junctions and combine with O2 to form
O2 − (Fig. 29C and D), thus decreasing the recombination rate of electron-hole pairs.

Additionally, the multicomponent heterojunction photocatalysts have been applied in the photocatalytic H2 evolution. Hsu and coworkers successfully synthesized Ag2S-coupled ZnO@ZnS core–shell nanorod heterostructures (Fig. 30 A) [53]. The maximum H2 evolution rate for Ag2S/ZnO@ZnS multicomponent heterojunction reached 5870 μmol g−1 h−1 under UV light (Fig. 30B). The increased photocurrent of Ag2S/ZnO@ZnS multicomponent heterojunction indicated effective separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Fig. 30C). The amount of H2 evolution for the fifth cycle slightly decreased by about 12%, in comparison with that of the first cycle (Fig. 30D). Thus, this work provides a new strategy to fabricate relatively robust photocatalysts for efficient H2 evolution (Fig. 30E).

In another example, Islam et al. synthesized MoS2/BiOI/AgI double Z-scheme multicomponent heterojunction via a simple precipitation process [228]. The improved charge transfer and separation result in the admirable photocatalytic performance of MoS2/BiOI/AgI. In this multicomponent heterojunction system, the electrons transfer from the CB of MoS2 and AgI to the CB of BiOI, whereas the holes move from the VB of BiOI to the VB of AgI and MoS2. Finally, the electrons from the BiOI combine with the holes in the VB of the AgI and MoS2 (Fig. 31 A). Apparently, this multicomponent heterojunction improves the separation of electron-hole pairs in this system, thus leading to a higher photocatalytic performance. The optimum photocatalytic performance of MoS2/BiOI/AgI (containing 2 wt% of MoS2) showed the highest RhB degradation rate of 6.46 × 10−2 min−1, which is almost 7 and 16 times higher than those of BiOI/AgI and BiOI (Fig. 31B and C) [228].

Wang and coworkers successfully synthesized “cauline leaf”-like 3D TiO2@MoS2@CdS multicomponent heterojunctions (Fig. 32 A–C) [353]. The maximum photocatalytic H2-evolution rate over TiO2@MoS2@CdS was 6.2 mmol h−1 g−1 under visible light, with an apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of 57.6% at 420 nm (Fig. 32D). The “cauline leaf”-like structure could enhance light trapping and absorption, offer conductive bridge for rapid charge separation and transport to achieve efficient separation of electron-hole pairs, and provide high surface area for producing H2-evolution reaction. The TiO2 and CdS were excited to generate electron-hole pairs, and then the electrons immediately transferred to the surface of MoS2 for H2 production. The remaining holes in the VB of TiO2 and CdS would be consumed by sacrificial reagent (Fig. 32E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of hierarchical porous photocatalysts

ce:section.ce:para: Although the hierarchical photocatalysts exhibit significantly increased surface area and active sites, the surface photocatalytic reaction kinetics are still slow in many cases, especially for the uphill reactions. Thus, the loading of suitable cocatalysts seems to be very necessary to further boost the photocatalytic activity through accelerating the sluggish reaction kinetics. So far, various cocatalysts, such as Au [354,355], Pt [356,357], Pt/PtO [364], Ag [209], Pd [361], Ti3C2 [362], Ni(OH)2 [363], CoPx [26,398], NiPx [399,400], NiSx [14,360], MoSe2 [365] and MoS2 [366,367] have been loaded in different hierarchical photocatalysts for enhanced photocatalysis. Typically, the noble metals, such as Pt, Au and Ag, are well-known cocatalysts for boosting the photocatalytic activity for H2 evolution, O2 and CO2 reduction. For example, Domen et al. demonstrated that nanoporous CdS nanosheets and hollow nanorods could be prepared at room temperature through the formation of self-templating Cd(OH)2 intermediates and subsequent S2−/OH− ion-exchange conversion [401]. The resulting CdS photocatalysts could achieve an apparent quantum yield of about 60.3% at 420 nm through loading monodisperse 3–5 nm Pt nanocrystals. Similarly, the deposition of plasmonic Au cocatalysts on the surface of ZnS flower-like nanoarchitectures could greatly enhance their H2-evolution activity [402]. Additionally, H2-evolution over ZnIn2S4 microspheres could also be significantly improved through loading the carbon quantum dots and Pt as dual cocatalysts [403].

However, the high costs and low abundance of noble metals limit their large-scale photocatalytic applications. Thus, here, we will highlight the earth-abundant metal-based cocatalysts for different applications. For example, CuS (2 mol%) loaded ZnS porous nanosheets can obtain a H2-production quantum efficiency of 20% at 420 nm [404]. The enhanced visible-light H2-evolution activity is closely associated with the synergistic effect of the photoinduced interfacial charge transfer (IFCT) from the VB of ZnS to CuS and the low-cost CuS/Cu2S cocatalysts. In another example, Ni(OH)2-modified TiO2 nanofibers showed significantly enhanced photocatalytic CO2 reduction performance compared to pure TiO2 (Fig. 34 A) [363]. The maximum methane-production rate of TiO2/Ni(OH)2(0.5 wt%) was 2.20 µmol h−1 g−1 (Fig. 34A). The loading of Ni(OH)2 serves three purposes: (1) improving the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, (2) enhancing CO2 capture capacity, and (3) altering the product selectivity (Fig. 34B) [363].

Chen et al. demonstrated that loading NiS2 nanoparticles as cocatalyst could significantly enhanced photocatalytic H2-evolution performance over g-C3N4 nanosheets (Fig. 35 A–C) [360]. The g-C3N4 with 3% NiS2 nanoparticles displayed the highest H2-generation rate (116.3 µmol h−1 g−1). The results further confirm that loading NiS2 nanoparticles could improve separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, act as H2-generation active sits and enhance H2-evolution kinetics during the photocatalytic process (Fig. 35D–F).

Furthermore, in order to achieve efficient separation of charge and improve both surface reductive and oxidative half-reactions, Qin et al. constructed NiCoP-based core/shell cocatalyst to improve photocatalytic H2 production over g-C3N4 via a facile solid-state phosphorization reaction (Fig. 36 A) [405]. According to Mott-Schottky test, the flat band potential for NiCoP and NiCoP@NiCo-Pi were −0.08 and −0.05 eV, which was higher than that of g-C3N4/NiCoP@NiCo-Pi. Furthermore, the redox potentials of Ni3+/Ni2+ and Co3+/Co2+ were more positive than that of TEOA+/TEOA. Therefore, the photogenerated electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 could transfer to the Fermi level of NiCoP for proton reduction to generate H2. The nickel(II) and cobalt(II) ions in NiCo-Pi could capture photoinduced holes from the VB of g-C3N4 to form high-valence nickel and cobalt ions, resulting in the efficient separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. More importantly, the holes in NiCo-Pi could be effectively consumed by TEOA (Fig. 36B and C). Thus, the g-C3N4/NiCoP@NiCo-Pi showed an optimized photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 534.2 μmol g−1 h−1, which was 1.3 times higher than that of g-C3N4/NiCoP photocatalysts. Therefore, the NiCoP@NiCo-Pi core/shell cocatalyst could efficiently enhance the charge separation and accelerate both surface reductive and oxidation reactions (Fig. 36D).

Liu and coworkers demonstrated that Bi2MO6 loaded with SnO2 as cocatalyst could achieve 97% degradation ratio for RhB and 90% for nitrobenzene under visible light irradiation [406]. The maximum rate constants k value of SnO2/Bi2MO6 was 0.00615 min−1, which was 20 times higher than that of pure Bi2MO6 (0.00031 min−1) (Fig. 37 A). The improved photocatalytic degradation performance is mainly attributed to the efficient separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers and more active sites due to the suitable energy levels and good interfacial contact between SnO2 and Bi2MoO6 (Fig. 37B–D). Under visible light, the excited Bi2MoO6 could generate the electron-hole pairs. Since the position of CB of SnO2 is more positive than that of Bi2MoO6, the photogenerated electrons would transfer from the CB of Bi2MoO6 to the CB of SnO2, thus leading to an efficient separation of the photocatalytic electron-hole pairs (Fig. 37E).

Additionally, MoS2 as non-noble metal cocatalyst has been intensively loaded onto different hierarchical photocatalysts to improve their photocatalytic activities [279,357,407–413]. In future, it is expected that other earth-abundant cocatalysts (i.e., NiS x
[14,29,169,414,415], WC [416], Ni3C [13,28], Ni(OH)2 [417] and phosphides [26,27,400,418–421]), spatially separated dual cocatalysts [422–425] and metal-free cocatalysts (such as graphene [426–429] and carbon quantum dots [117,430,431]) could be loaded on the hierarchical photocatalysts to further enhance their photocatalytic performances.

In general, exposing the reactive facets of hierarchical photocatalysts could not only promote the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, but also could tune the adsorption selectivity of charged species and reaction activity of photocatalysts. For anatase TiO2, the (0 0 1) facets are more reactive than the (1 0 1) facets, but anatase TiO2 crystals are usually dominated by (1 0 1) facets [432]. Furthermore, it is known that the (0 0 1) facets of TiO2 are an oxidative facet, whereas (1 0 0) and (1 0 1) facets are reductive facets [433–435]. More importantly, exposing the reactive facets could also favor the precise loading of single or dual cocatalysts in the proper locations, thus achieving significantly improved facet-dependent photocatalytic performance [154,425,436].

The promoted charge separation and reactivity through exposing the reactive facets have been widely reported [111,369,370,437–439]. The exposed highly reactive facets generally exhibit different atomic arrangement and electronic structures, which could provide more active sites for the higher adsorption of the reactants, and accumulation of the specific photogenerated electrons or holes, thus fundamentally boosting the charge separation and subsequent surface photocatalytic reactions [369,439]. As a typical example, Yu’s group demonstrated that a surface heterojunction in single TiO2 particle could be created between the co-exposed (0 0 1) and (1 0 1) facets of anatase TiO2 due to their significantly different band structures and CB/VB positions [440]. According to the proposed electron overflow effect on the (1 0 1) facets based on DFT calculations (Fig. 38 A), it is easily found that the anatase TiO2 with the optimal ratio of the exposed (0 0 1) and (1 0 1) facets (45:55) could show the highest CH4-production activity from CO2 photoreduction (Fig. 38B) [440]. Inspired by this excellent example, the surface heterojunction of different semiconductors with co-exposed facets have attracted extensive attention [441,442], which are expected to be deeply investigated in near future.

In general, it should be noted that the formation of hierarchical nanostructures does not have a significant impact on the band gaps of semiconductors. Thereby, measures need to be taken to manipulate the electronic structure, efficiently decrease the band gaps, enhance the visible-light absorption efficiency, and tune surface acidity/basicity, dissociative adsorption, conductivity, reactivity and other chemical properties. Many metal ions (e.g., Fe3+ [392,444–447], Cu2+ [448–450], Zn2+ [451–453], Ni2+ [454,455], In3+ [456], Bi3+ [457,458], Sn4+ [459,460], Ti3+ [461–464], Mo6+ [465] and Nb5+ [466]), nonmetals (e.g., N [351,388,442,467], C [116,468,469], O [381], S [470,471], F [472], Br [388], Cl [472], P [473] and I [445,469,473]) and vacancies, or point defects [474–478] have been intensively introduced into various hierarchical photocatalysts to narrow wide band gaps and promote the charge separation. For example, the surface fluorination and fluorine-doping of TiO2 can enhance its photocatalytic activity for pollutant degradation due to the increased mobility of OH radicals on hierarchical F-TiO2 surface [479–484]. Furthermore, the increased density of the surface vacancy such as oxygen on hierarchical photocatalysts could also efficiently promote the charge separation efficiencies and photoredox reactions [139,485–487].

Introducing heteroatoms is a usually employed approach in photocatalytic CO2 reduction. Fu et al. successfully synthesized O-doped g-C3N4 nanotube via thermal exfoliation and condensation of bulk g-C3N4 (Fig. 41 A and B). The methanol-generation rate of O-doped g-C3N4 nanotube (0.88 µmol g−1 h−1) is five times higher than that of pure g-C3N4 (0.17 µmol g−1 h−1) (Fig. 41C). The improved photocatalytic performance of O-doped g-C3N4 nanotube is ascribed to the O-doping effect and hierarchical nanotube structure (Fig. 34). The O-doping optimizes the band gap of g-C3N4 (Fig. 41D), increases the CO2 affinity, and improves the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [381].

Furthermore, N-doped TiO2 with 35% (1 0 1) and 65% (0 0 1) exposed facets also shows admirable performance in photocatalytic CO2 reduction [368]. C-doped TiO2 is also believed to enhance the photocatalytic degradation performance. Zhang et al. successfully synthesized SiO2/C-doped TiO2 hollow spheres via a template method (Fig. 42 A and B). After doping C into TiO2, the UV–vis absorption of SiO2/C-doped TiO2 not only displayed a red shift, but also showed a stronger absorbance in the region of 200–400 nm (Fig. 42C). The C doping into the TiO2 could introduce localized occupied states in the TiO2 lattice, which narrowed the band gap to ∼3.14 eV, thus leading to a significantly improved photocatalytic performance (Fig. 42D). As a result, the UV–vis absorption of SiO2/C-doped TiO2 shows a better photocatalytic degradation performance than P25 under visible-light irradiation (Fig. 42E). The SiO2/C-doped TiO2 could degrade 93.6% RhB within 60 min, whereas the RhB degradation by P25 was only 48.8% [63].

In another example, it was found that the Cu-doped ZnS solid-solution hollow nanomaterials exhibited very high H2-evolution rate under xenon lamp irradiation, which was 6 and 130 folds higher than that of pure “ZnS-shell” and co-precipitated ZnS particles, respectively [488]. Importantly, the Cu doping also achieved the visible-light H2-evolution activity. Furthermore, the Bi-doped ZnS hollow spheres also show greatly enhanced UV and visible-light photocatalytic H2-production activity [489]. It was believed that the promoted separation and suppressed recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs were responsible for the significant photoactivity enhancement due to the Bi doping.

To efficiently utilize the visible light, a number of doped TiO2 photocatalysts with hierarchical structures have been developed. For example, Fe-doped TiO2 porous microspheres have been proven to exhibit excellent activity for MB photodegradation under visible light [490]. Furthermore, a high percentage of (0 0 1) facets of Fe-doped TiO2 nanosheet building blocks could promote the dissociation of the adsorbed MB molecules, thus further enhancing their photoactivity of MB degradation [491]. Apart from metal cation doping, non-metal doping has been considered to be one promising strategy to improve the visible-light activity of hierarchical TiO2 [492]. Fan and co-workers studied the influence of (N, I) co-doping on the photocatalytic degradation of MB by biogenic TiO2 under visible light [493]. They proved that the I and N co-doping could induce a clear red shift of band gap absorption edge, thus achieving the excellent photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation. Similarly, the N-F co-doping could efficiently enhance the photocatalytic degradation of MO over mesopores TiO2 nanobelts [494].

Additionally, adding photosensitizer has been demonstrated to be an effective strategy to boost the overall photocatalytic efficiency due to the increased light harvesting and number of photogenerated charge carriers for photocatalysis. For instance, Zhang and coworkers fabricated the Pd-Bi2WO6 photocatalyst via a hydrothermal and chemical deposition method (Fig. 43 A). The results demonstrated that Bi2WO6-2%Pd exhibited complete degradation of 0.01 g/L phenol within one hour (Fig. 43B). This phenomenon is attributed to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect, which could not only serve as the electron receptor, but also improved the visible light absorption, thus leading to decrease of the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and the generation of more photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Fig. 43C and D). As a result, more photogenerated holes were retained on the surface of Bi2WO6, thus causing the significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance [302].

Cai and coworkers successfully synthesized L-ZnO/S,N:GQD (sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots) [382]. After loading S,N:GQD, the L-ZnO/S,N:GQD showed higher light absorption than L-ZnO, which is attributed to the S,N:GQDs coupling (Fig. 44 A). This result indicates that the S,N:GQDs improve the visible light absorbance of the L-ZnO. The role of S,N:GQDs was further confirmed by the photoelectrochemical performances. The photocurrent of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD is much higher than that of L-ZnO, indicating an improved separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Fig. 44B). The resulting L-ZnO/S,N:GQD displayed higher photocatalytic degradation performance than that of L-ZnO (Fig. 44C). Accordingly, the excellent photocatalytic degradation performance of L-ZnO/S,N:GQD is attributed to two factors of S,N:GQD (Fig. 44D).

So far, although much progress has been made in the surface and interface engineering of hierarchical photocatalysts, many challenges still remain in facilely fabricating these hierarchical heterojunction photocatalysts, performing proper surface modification and deeply understanding their photocatalytic enhancement mechanisms. In this review, we first systematically discussed challenges in advanced hierarchical photocatalysts, then thoroughly summarized the important interface engineering strategies and different surface modification approaches of hierarchical photocatalysts. In each specific section, some important advancements and typical examples are discussed.

Among the various types of interface engineering strategies of hierarchical nanostructured photocatalysts, the hierarchical direct Z-scheme heterojunctions based on multi-dimensional hybridization of building blocks deserve more attention in future, due to their favorable charge separation and enhanced redox capacity. Especially, creating hierarchical carbon-based heterojunctions seems to be more appealing for the photocatalysis enhancement due to the low cost and superior properties of nanocarbon materials. In addition, designing hierarchical multicomponent heterojunctions also holds great promise in photocatalysis because of the combinational effects of multicomponent heterojunctions. In future, the development of multicomponent heterojunctions based on nanocarbons and direct Z-scheme heterojunctions may open an exciting avenue to accelerate their extensive applications in hierarchical photocatalysis.

Although a variety of surface modification approaches have been available, it is still challenging in constructing the exposed reactive facets and loading suitable cocatalysts. Cocatalysts with high electrocatalytic activity and low cost are still desirable. At this point, the spatially separated dual cocatalysts, earth-abundant 2D layered and metal-free nanocarbon cocatalysts could be deposited on the hierarchical photocatalysts to further optimize photocatalytic performances. More importantly, the stability of cocatalysts should be solved urgently in future. Additionally, the combination of different surface modification strategies should be widely investigated in future studies.

More importantly, the fundamental enhancement mechanisms of hierarchical heterojunction photocatalysts with the proper surface modification strategies should be explored by suitable techniques, particularly in situ characterization techniques. It is critical to identify the exact catalytic sites, the charge transfer pathways, and the formation of reactive oxygen species (such as
OH,
O2 − and H2O2) and surface reaction selectivity, which will benifit the rational design and construction of high-quality multifunctional hierarchical photocatalysts. We hope that the present review will stimulate scientific interest in the design and surface modification of hierarchical heterojunctions for various environment- and energy-related applications.
